did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses end-to-end object scale-oriented fully convolutional neural networks (OSO-FCNs) based on the VGG-16 architecture for the task of skin lesion segmentation in dermoscopic images. The method involves fine-tuning the pre-trained VGG-16 model on the ISIC2016 dataset to produce pixel-level masks for melanoma and other skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL PAPER' and presents a novel method (OSO-FCNs) for skin lesion segmentation. It includes original experimental results, comparisons with state-of-the-art algorithms on the ISIC2016 dataset, and detailed methodology sections, indicating it produces new results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin lesion segmentation using object scale-oriented fully convolutional neural networks
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin lesion segmentation in dermoscopic images to aid in the early diagnosis of melanoma. The authors propose an end-to-end object scale-oriented fully convolutional network (OSO-FCN) based on the VGG-16 architecture, which utilizes a novel OSO training strategy to account for lesion scale variations. The method was trained and evaluated on the ISIC2016 dataset, which contains 900 training and 379 testing images with ground truth annotations. Performance was assessed using metrics including sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, precision, Jaccard index, and F measure. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed OSO-FCN achieved a segmentation accuracy of 95.8% and sensitivity of 92.5%, outperforming or matching state-of-the-art methods. The approach is characterized by its simplicity, lack of complex post-processing, and high efficiency.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on skin lesion segmentation to aid in the early diagnosis of melanoma. The ISIC2016 dataset used contains images of melanoma (and other lesions) which are segmented as a binary mask (lesion vs. skin). Figure 1b shows an example of malignant melanoma.

The study includes benign nevi in the segmentation task. Figure 1a shows an example of a benign nevi from the ISIC2016 database. The segmentation task treats all pigmented skin lesions (including nevi and melanoma) as the foreground class against the skin background.

The primary target of the segmentation algorithm is 'pigmented skin lesions' generally. The method produces a pixel-level mask for 'skin lesion areas' without distinguishing specific histological types during the segmentation process itself, treating them as a single class of interest against normal skin.

all_task_types.target: Pixel-level mask for skin lesion areas
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes end-to-end object scale-oriented fully convolutional networks (OSO-FCNs) to produce a pixel-level mask for skin lesion areas in dermoscopic images. The task is binary segmentation (lesion vs. skin/background), evaluated using metrics such as Jaccard index, sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, precision, and F measure on the ISIC2016 dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC2016
datasets_used.description: A public skin lesion database released by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) for the International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI) challenges in 2016. It contains dermoscopic images with ground truth annotations manually segmented by experienced dermatologists.

use.training: Used for fine-tuning the pre-trained VGG-16 model into fully convolutional networks (FCNs) using the proposed object scale-oriented (OSO) training strategy. The training dataset was divided into 2 subsets based on object occupation ratio to train 3 different scale-oriented FCNs.
use.testing: Used for evaluating segmentation performance (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, etc.) and comparing with state-of-the-art methods.
use.other: The pre-trained VGG-16 model was originally trained on the ImageNet Large-Scale Visual Recognition Challenge (ILSVRC2014) dataset, which was used for initial feature extraction before adaptation to the ISIC2016 dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, precision, Jaccard index, F measure) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC2016.notes: The OSO-FCNs achieved the highest accuracy (95.8%) and sensitivity (92.5%). The Jaccard index of 0.845 was very close to JD-FCNs (0.847). The F measure of 0.911 was nearly the same as the best F measure of 0.912 achieved by JD-FCNs and MS-FCNs. ALL-FCNs performed worse than OSO-FCNs on all indices. The average segmentation calculation time for each test sample was approximately 100 ms.

architecture_used.main_model: Object Scale-Oriented Fully Convolutional Networks (OSO-FCNs) based on VGG-16
architecture_used.pretraining: VGG-16 pre-trained on ImageNet Large-Scale Visual Recognition Challenge (ILSVRC2014)
architecture_used.input_size: Height scaled to 256 pixels; width adaptively scaled maintaining aspect ratio (original size 1022x767 resized to 342x256 in example)

architecture_details.modifications: Fully connected layers of VGG-16 transformed into three convolutional layers; last 1000-way classification layer replaced with 2-way convolutional layer
architecture_details.structure: Six stacks of convolution layers (Cmn) and a stack of deconvolution layers (DCx). Lower five stacks have 3x3 convolution filters followed by ReLU. Max pooling layers (Pi) between stacks. After fifth pooling layer (P5), three fully connected layers are transformed. Three FCNs constructed: FCNs32, FCNs16, and FCNs8. FCNs32 uses final convolution layer (C63). FCNs16 combines C63 with P4. FCNs8 combines C63 with P3 and P4.
architecture_details.layers: Convolution layers with 3x3 filters, ReLU activation, Max Pooling layers, Deconvolution layers (DC2, DC4, DC8, DC16, DC32)
architecture_details.heads: 2-way convolutional layer for binary segmentation (lesion vs. skin)
architecture_details.classifier_head: 2-way convolutional layer replacing the original 1000-way classification layer

training_details.loss_function: Sum of cross-entropy terms over each pixel in the output layer
training_details.training_schedule: 100,000 iterations per network; approximately 8 hours training time per network
training_details.training_strategy: Object Scale-Oriented (OSO) training: Training dataset divided into Train_big and Train_small based on object occupation ratio (R_obj). Three networks trained: FCNs32A (on all data), FCNs8B (on Train_big), FCNs8S (on Train_small)

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing: Height scaled to 256 pixels, width adaptively scaled maintaining aspect ratio (e.g., 1022x767 resized to 342x256)
Ground truth reformatting: Background pixels set to 0, lesion pixels set to 1, edge pixels set to 255 (ignored during training)
conclusions: The proposed end-to-end object scale-oriented fully convolutional networks (OSO-FCNs) achieve high segmentation accuracy for pigmented skin lesions, with an accuracy of 95.8% and sensitivity of 92.5% on the ISIC2016 testing set.

The OSO-FCN method outperforms or performs very close to state-of-the-art methods (including EXB, CUMED, Mahmudur, JD-FCNs, MS-FCNs, and ALL-FCNs) in terms of segmentation metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, Jaccard index, and F measure.

The scale of lesions in the training dataset significantly affects segmentation results; specifically, a large object occupation ratio in the training set correlates with larger object occupation ratios in the segmentation results of the test set.

The proposed OSO training strategy, which divides the training dataset into subsets based on object occupation ratio to train scale-oriented networks, effectively addresses the issue of lesion scale variation.
The method is characterized by its simplicity, requiring only simple preprocessing (image resizing and format conversion) and no complex post-processing steps.
The approach demonstrates high efficiency, with an average segmentation calculation time of approximately 100 ms per test sample.
Using a coarse classification network trained on the entire dataset to estimate the scale of test objects improves the accuracy of scale estimation and subsequent segmentation.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC2016 dataset for both training and testing, with no external validation on independent datasets, limiting generalizability assessment.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the superiority of the proposed OSO-FCNs over state-of-the-art methods; comparisons are based solely on descriptive metrics.
The hardware used for experiments (Intel Core2 Q8300, 8 GB RAM, NVIDIA GeForce GTX Titan X) is outdated, which may affect reproducibility and efficiency claims in modern contexts.
The method treats all pigmented skin lesions (melanoma and benign nevi) as a single foreground class, lacking histological specificity in the segmentation output.

The OSO training strategy introduces complexity by requiring a coarse classification step to estimate object scale before final segmentation, potentially increasing inference time compared to simpler end-to-end models.

Data leakage risk is present as the pre-trained VGG-16 weights were initialized from ImageNet (ILSVRC2014), which may bias feature extraction towards natural images rather than medical dermoscopic images.
The paper does not report confidence intervals or standard deviations for the reported metrics, making it difficult to assess the variance and robustness of the results.
